questions.question: Which of the following is true about the size of an array compared to an ArrayList?
options: Both arrays and ArrayLists have a fixed size once created
Arrays have a fixed size, while ArrayLists can grow and shrink dynamically
Arrays can grow dynamically, while ArrayLists have a fixed size
Both arrays and ArrayLists resize automatically when elements are added
questions.answer: Arrays have a fixed size, while ArrayLists can grow and shrink dynamically

questions.question: Which data types can an array hold that an ArrayList cannot?
options: String and Object types
Wrapper class types such as Integer and Double
Primitive types such as int, double, and boolean
Arrays can hold all the same types as ArrayList
questions.answer: Primitive types such as int, double, and boolean

questions.question: How do you access the third element of an array named 'data'?
options: data.get(3)
data[3]
data[2]
data.get(2)
questions.answer: data[2]

questions.question: How do you access the third element of an ArrayList named 'data'?
options: data[2]
data.get(3)
data.get(2)
data.element(2)
questions.answer: data.get(2)

questions.question: What is the correct way to get the number of elements in an array versus an ArrayList?
options: arr.length() for arrays and list.size() for ArrayLists
arr.size() for arrays and list.length for ArrayLists
arr.length for arrays and list.size() for ArrayLists
arr.count() for arrays and list.count() for ArrayLists
questions.answer: arr.length for arrays and list.size() for ArrayLists

questions.question: Why is 'length' a property (no parentheses) for arrays but 'size()' is a method for ArrayLists?
options: There is no difference; both can be used with or without parentheses
Arrays use a field because their size is fixed and stored directly, while ArrayList uses a method because it is a class with encapsulated behavior
It is a Java naming convention with no technical reason behind it
Arrays use a method too, but the parentheses are optional in Java
questions.answer: Arrays use a field because their size is fixed and stored directly, while ArrayList uses a method because it is a class with encapsulated behavior

questions.question: Which of the following correctly converts an array to an ArrayList?
options: ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(arr);
ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(arr));
ArrayList<String> list = ArrayList.fromArray(arr);
ArrayList<String> list = (ArrayList<String>) arr;
questions.answer: ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(arr));

questions.question: Which of the following correctly converts an ArrayList to an array?
options: String[] arr = list.toArray(new String[0]);
String[] arr = (String[]) list;
String[] arr = list.convertToArray();
String[] arr = Arrays.fromList(list);
questions.answer: String[] arr = list.toArray(new String[0]);

questions.question: When is an array the better choice over an ArrayList?
options: When you need to frequently add and remove elements
When you do not know the number of elements in advance
When working with fixed-size data, primitives, or performance-critical code
When you need built-in search and sort methods
questions.answer: When working with fixed-size data, primitives, or performance-critical code

questions.question: What is the default value stored in each element of a newly created int array?
options: null
The array is empty and has no default values

questions.question: What is the default value stored in each element of a newly created String array?
options: An empty string ""
null
"null"
The element does not exist until assigned
questions.answer: null

questions.question: What exception is thrown when you access an array index that does not exist?
options: IndexOutOfRangeException
ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
InvalidIndexException
NullPointerException
questions.answer: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException

questions.question: What happens when you assign one array variable to another, as in 'int[] b = a;'?
options: A deep copy of the array is created
Both variables reference the same array object in memory
Only the first element is copied
A compile-time error occurs
questions.answer: Both variables reference the same array object in memory

questions.question: Which method creates an independent copy of an array?
options: Arrays.clone(original)
Arrays.copyOf(original, original.length)
original.copy()
new int[](original)
questions.answer: Arrays.copyOf(original, original.length)

questions.question: What is autoboxing in Java?
options: Converting an object to a primitive type automatically
Automatically wrapping a primitive value into its corresponding wrapper class object
Casting one object type to another
Creating a new ArrayList from an array automatically
questions.answer: Automatically wrapping a primitive value into its corresponding wrapper class object

questions.question: Given 'ArrayList<Integer> nums = new ArrayList<>(); nums.add(10); nums.add(20); nums.add(30);', what does nums.remove(1) do?
options: Removes the element with value 1
Removes the element at index 1, which is 20
Removes the first element, which is 10
Throws an IndexOutOfBoundsException
questions.answer: Removes the element at index 1, which is 20

questions.question: How do you remove the value 10 (not the element at index 10) from an ArrayList<Integer>?
options: nums.remove(10)
nums.delete(10)
nums.remove(Integer.valueOf(10))
nums.removeValue(10)
questions.answer: nums.remove(Integer.valueOf(10))

questions.question: What exception is thrown if you remove elements from an ArrayList using an enhanced for loop while iterating?
options: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
IllegalStateException
ConcurrentModificationException
UnsupportedOperationException
questions.answer: ConcurrentModificationException

questions.question: What is the safe way to remove elements from an ArrayList while iterating?
options: Use an enhanced for loop and call remove() inside it
Use a standard for loop iterating backwards from the last index to zero
Convert the ArrayList to an array first, then remove from the array
Use a while loop that always starts at index 0
questions.answer: Use a standard for loop iterating backwards from the last index to zero

questions.question: What does the generic type syntax in 'ArrayList<String>' provide?
options: It allows the ArrayList to hold any type of object
It enforces type safety so only String objects can be added to the list
It makes the ArrayList immutable
It specifies the initial capacity of the ArrayList
questions.answer: It enforces type safety so only String objects can be added to the list

questions.question: What happens if you use a raw ArrayList without specifying a generic type?
options: The code will not compile
The ArrayList can hold any type of object but you lose compile-time type checking
The ArrayList defaults to holding Integer objects
The program throws a runtime exception when any element is added
questions.answer: The ArrayList can hold any type of object but you lose compile-time type checking

questions.question: When you pass an array to a method in Java, what is actually passed?
options: A complete copy of the array
Only the first element of the array
A reference to the array, so changes inside the method affect the original
An immutable view of the array
questions.answer: A reference to the array, so changes inside the method affect the original

questions.question: Which of the following correctly sorts an ArrayList<String> named 'items' alphabetically?
options: items.sort()
Arrays.sort(items)
Collections.sort(items)
items.orderBy()
questions.answer: Collections.sort(items)

questions.question: Which statement about ArrayList is FALSE?
options: ArrayList can store null values
ArrayList can hold primitive types directly without wrapper classes
ArrayList provides a built-in toString() method that prints elements in bracket format
ArrayList is part of the java.util package
questions.answer: ArrayList can hold primitive types directly without wrapper classes

questions.question: You need to store exactly 12 monthly temperature readings and performance is critical. Which is the better choice and why?
options: ArrayList<Double>, because it provides more built-in methods
A double[] array, because the size is fixed and known, and arrays of primitives are faster than ArrayLists of wrapper objects
ArrayList<Double>, because it can resize if you add more months later
It makes no difference; both perform identically in all cases
questions.answer: A double[] array, because the size is fixed and known, and arrays of primitives are faster than ArrayLists of wrapper objects
